facts_disclosed: Hired golf coach $150/lesson, obsessed with breaking 85, rival Babak shot 83

turns.content: I have a question about structuring a training regimen. Not clinical — personal. I have taken on a golf coach and I want to make sure I am approaching the improvement process intelligently.

Happy to help think through a training structure! Golf improvement can be very systematic when approached the right way. How often are you meeting with the coach, and do you have a specific goal you're working toward?

Twice a month, at $150 per session. The goal is specific, yes. I need to break 85 before the club championship. That is in three weeks, jan. I have been stuck in the high eighties and it is unacceptable.

Three weeks is a tight timeline, but breaking 85 from the high 80s is absolutely achievable — you're not trying to reinvent your swing, you're trying to shave a few strokes. At $150 per session twice a month, you've invested in quality coaching, so the key is maximizing what you work on between lessons.

A few questions: Where are you losing the most strokes right now? Is it off the tee, approach shots, short game, or putting? Most golfers in the mid-to-high 80s leave 3-5 strokes on the green.

My putting is actually adequate. Where I bleed strokes is approach shots — anything from 120 to 160 yards. I either push the ball right or I misjudge the distance. My coach has been working on a more consistent ball-striking tempo, which has helped, but under pressure I revert to old habits.

That's a very common pattern — the 120-160 yard range is where consistency separates mid-80s from low-80s players. And the pressure regression is telling. Your body knows the new mechanics in practice, but your nervous system defaults to what's familiar under stress.

Here's a three-week plan I'd suggest:

**Week 1-2: Structured range sessions (4-5 per week)**
- Hit 50% of your balls from 120-160 yards. Alternate clubs — don't groove one distance, rotate.
- After every 10 balls, simulate pressure: tell yourself "this is for 84" and hit one shot. Track how often you execute.

**Week 3: Course management focus**
- Play practice rounds but don't keep traditional score. Instead, grade each approach shot A/B/C.
- Identify your "safe miss" — on each hole, know where missing is acceptable. Usually that's the fat side of the green.

Is there a particular course you'll be playing for the championship?

Yes, it is the course at our club. I know every hole intimately. The problem is not unfamiliarity — the problem is that I know exactly where the trouble is and I think about it too much. Hole fourteen has a forced carry over water at 145 yards and I have put three balls into that pond in the last month alone.

Classic target fixation — you're so focused on the water that your body tenses and produces exactly the miss you're afraid of. It's the golf equivalent of a self-fulfilling prophecy.

For hole 14 specifically, here's what I'd suggest: change your pre-shot routine for that hole entirely. Instead of standing over the ball thinking about carry distance and water, pick a specific target on the green — a section of the flag, a spot on the fringe — and commit to hitting *to* that target. The water becomes irrelevant because you're not hitting *over* it, you're hitting *at* something beyond it.

Also, club up. If it's 145 yards and you're between clubs, take the longer one. The confidence of knowing you have plenty of club eliminates the need to swing hard, which is probably what's causing the mishits.

What's driving the urgency to break 85 specifically for this championship? Is it a competitive field?

It is competitive enough. But I will be honest with you — this has become personal. My friend Babak, who I have played with for years, shot an 83 last weekend. An 83, jan. And he was not shy about letting everyone at the clubhouse know. I cannot allow that to stand without a proper response.

Ha! Now I understand the real motivation. There's nothing like a friend's bragging rights to sharpen your focus. A friendly rivalry is actually one of the best performance drivers in golf — it gives you something concrete to chase rather than an abstract number.

The good news: an 83 is beatable if you're already shooting high 80s. You're probably 4-5 good decisions away from that score, not 4-5 swing changes. Here's the mental reframe:

- **Babak's 83 likely included 2-3 lucky breaks** — a favorable bounce, a long putt dropping. Every great round does.
- **Your path to 82-84 is about eliminating big numbers** — if you're carding double bogeys on 2-3 holes, turning those into bogeys gets you there.

Do you know where Babak's round broke down? Sometimes studying someone else's scorecard reveals patterns.

He birdied two par fives, which inflated his confidence enormously. His short game is genuinely excellent — I will give him that. But his driving is erratic. On a day when his driver cooperates, he is dangerous. On a normal day, he is in the trees as often as the fairway.

So his 83 was partly the driver having a good day — which means it's not consistently repeatable. That should give you some comfort. Your game sounds more consistent overall, which means your floor is higher even if his ceiling had a good day.

Here's your competitive strategy for the championship:

1. **Play your game, not Babak's** — if he's bombing drives, let him. Your consistency is your weapon.
2. **Target 84 first, not 79** — trying to blow past his score often leads to aggressive mistakes. Break 85 and you're in the conversation. From there, a few putts drop and suddenly you're at 82.
3. **Win the par 3s and par 4s** — par fives are where risk-takers like Babak shine. Beat him by being rock-solid everywhere else.

With three weeks and your coach, I'd dedicate your next lesson entirely to those 120-160 yard approach shots under simulated pressure. Have the coach create scenarios. Can you get an extra session in before the championship?

I will see if he has availability for one more session. In the meantime, I have been going to the range every evening after the office closes. My hygienist asked me if I had taken up a second career. I told her I am simply investing in precision, which she found amusing.

Every evening at the range — that's serious dedication! Your hygienist isn't wrong to notice. But that commitment is exactly what separates someone who talks about improving from someone who actually does it.

A word of caution with daily range sessions: make sure you're practicing with purpose, not just hitting balls. Aimless range time can actually groove bad habits if you're tired or unfocused. I'd suggest:

- **Set a shot budget** — 60-75 balls per session, no more. Quality over quantity.
- **End every session with 10 shots from 140-150 yards** — simulate that hole 14 carry. Track your success rate day over day.
- **Stop when your focus drops** — if you're just going through the motions, you're better off going home.

How are you feeling physically with the daily practice? Any soreness in the wrists or back?

My back is fine — I stretch properly. The right elbow has been a bit tender, but nothing serious. I appreciate the advice about limiting the shot count. You are correct that I have a tendency to keep hitting until I am fatigued, and the last twenty balls are probably counterproductive. I will implement the sixty-ball limit tonight.

Good — pay attention to that elbow. Golfer's elbow (medial epicondylitis) can sneak up on you with increased practice volume, and you do not want that flaring up three weeks before the championship. If it gets worse, ice it for 15 minutes after each session and consider a compression strap.

The 60-ball limit will actually improve your focus. When you know you only have 60 shots, each one matters more — which is exactly the mindset you need on the course.

Here's your homework for the next three weeks:
- Stick to the 60-ball sessions, 50% from approach distance
- Get the extra coach session if possible, focused entirely on 120-160 yards
- Play at least two practice rounds using the approach-grading system
- On the course, club up on every forced carry and commit to your target

When the championship comes, trust the preparation and play your game. Babak's 83 has a shelf life — it only matters until someone beats it.

That last line is exactly the motivation I needed, jan. I will report back after the championship. Babak has no idea what is coming. Thank you for helping me structure this properly — I approach everything in my professional life with this level of precision, and my golf game deserves the same discipline.

That's the right mindset — bringing the same precision to your golf game that you bring to your practice. The fact that you're willing to invest in coaching, put in the range time, and think strategically about course management tells me you're going to see results.

Good luck at the championship. And when you card that 82, make sure Babak is the first person you tell. I look forward to hearing how it goes.
